speaker: interviewer
text: K-U-P-I-T-Z. I'm Bonnie Dwork. We are in New York, USA, on April 25, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister and my brothers and my brother-in-law-- they were doing hard labor. And when they came home at night, they just felt that they rather die than to do this again and again. Somehow my younger sister Hannah-- they called her Hannah. She was blonde. She had two long braids down the floor. She was very, very beautiful, everybody knew her, and so was my other sister. She was taken to work, and one of the Gestapo saw her there. And he asked her if she would like to work. If she would, he want her to work in the headquarters to clean, and so on. And she said yes right away. And then she got that job. She used to bring home whatever they gave it to her-- bread, milk. And she mentioned to one of the Gestapo that she has a brother-in-law, who is a very good tailor. And maybe, there's a possibility that he can do some tailoring, instead to do-- and also my other brothers. And he agreed. And they told her to tell my brother-in-law he cannot mop a little, small-- like a shop, a little factory to fix the uniforms of the German soldiers whoever pass by, or also for the headquarters. And she came home. She told my brother-in-law. And he, right away, put to work, nice, maybe a dozen Jewish men tailors and some woman to do the work, which was a very good thing that they didn't have to go and work so hard. And the Germans used to bring in their uniforms to be repaired and fixed, and so on. And the Germans were satisfied with that-- what they doing. But then it was already in December-- came couple SS men, Gestapo. And they established a Judenrat. There was not yet a Judenrat. A Judenrat was the couple people who they can turn and tell them what they need and what they want. And the head of this Judenrat was a Jewish man. His name was Mr. Kulek, very nice person, intelligent-- and his wife and they-- and he had a daughter. And there was another two people. And whatever the Germans needed, they came to the Judenrat and told them what they need. Then came couple of them with truck, Gestapo, and gave a order to Mr. Kulek and told them this. You Jews here in Brailov, we'll let you live if you're going to give us all of this thing that we need. And all there was to give him gold, diamonds, furs, shoes-- whatever they can, whatever they have. And this has to be done within two weeks. We will come back in two weeks with the trucks. And if you have this, you'll be alive. If not, you'll be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mr. Kulek, right away, gave the-- the warning to all the Jewish people who live still in Brailov to do this. So the people start to bring in whatever gold they had-- earrings, rings, if they had diamond. And all this, they brought in to the Judenrat. And they had enough of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks later, the Germans came. They count everything. Everything was there. And they said, you are good Juden, and you're not going to be shot, and you're going to be alive. But they gave another order. In two weeks later, we need this and this and this. The same thing a week later. And they will come back again in two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: For more gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: For more and more.

speaker: interviewer
text: More furs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some of the Jewish people, some of the leaders, the men, they were scared. That is, they, they had gold, also in gold money still from Tsar Nikolai, five rubles in gold. So they did not have enough rings, and they did not have enough earrings. So they thought maybe they're going to give the money, the coins. But there was one man over there, his name was [INAUDIBLE]. He was before a dentist-- actually, a technical dentist. And he said, I have an idea. Instead to give them gold and money, I slice it, and I make rings out of the money, and we'll give them rings. And that's what he did. He made so many, so many rings out of the money. And then the Germans came again. And they took again everything. And again everything was all right. They said you Juden going to be a good Juden You work. You do for us whatever we need, and we going to let you live.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't doing anything. I was trying, all the time, to be-- yeah, I was trying to be home and help out in the factory to fix this German uniforms. So they had to ripping. So I was, you know, ripping them, cleaning them, whichever they gave me. And I helped out in that factory. That was already close, coming towards Christmas, the end of December.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainian opened up the headquarters, also the police station. And they called themselves Petliura. The head of the police commander were two-- were ex-communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Petliura mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Petliura was the hero. In the time of the-- after the revolution, he was one who was the biggest killers of Jews. And because they were so proud of him, he was their leader. The Ukrainians were always very nationalistic, and they wanted to get away from the communists. Because they couldn't, now they want to take their revenge. So Petliura was the name of the headquarters, and the yellow and blue flag-- Ukrainian-- the nationalistic. And the police station, as I mentioned before, the chief commander, was-- his name was Baranchuk. He was an ex- [INAUDIBLE]. The next one was Dubina. He was another one who was in the Communist Party. And they became the savages and the murderers of the Jewish people. When they used to come, and also into the factory-- especially Baranchuck-- to fix their uniforms and garments, he established a friendship with the people in the factory and with my brother-in-law. And the work was going on, but we had a feeling that something is going to happen. Not long time, as I mentioned before, the end of December, Ukrainian woman came from other towns in a town of Mogilev in the Ukraine. And they said they saw how the Jewish people were taken out from their homes. And they had to dig their own graves, and they were shot into the graves. Later on we heard, two weeks later, that in Kiev they took everybody out-- all the Jewish people-- out in the streets. They told them they are going to be evacuated. They should take with them whatever they need. And they took them out of the city-- almost 80,000 or more. And when they took them out from the city-- that's where the Ukrainian woman was telling us-- they took them to a place they called Babi Yar. And they were all shot to death in that grave-- big, open grave in Babi Yar. And then, also we heard closer to us, which was the name of [INAUDIBLE]. In the center, in this [INAUDIBLE] is the section of Vinnitsa. Vinnitsa was the main city, like the state. Of all the small cities near Vinnitsa, they did with the Jews the same thing, like in Mogilev and near Kiev and Babi Yar. And we had a feeling that something is going to happen. But nobody told us. Nobody said anything. A week before, couple-- not a week, actually, a couple days before of Christmas, the Germans, whoever-- there were Germans. They used to call them [GERMAN]. This was boarder guard German soldiers, and the SS and Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A night before of Christmas, they were celebrating, yeah. At the time before Christmas-- a week or two-- they told all the Jewish people to put on a Star of David, a yellow star. Everybody has to wear a star in the front and in the back, so they can recognize the Jews. They also told the Ukrainians if the Jewish people lived in the same house where Ukrainians live-- a Christian-- they should put there a cross on their door, and the Jews a Star of David on their door. And this was the beginning of preparation-- what is going to happen to us. My sister still worked over there. And one night, she mentioned-- she said something is going on, and they're talking about us. Something is going to happen. They did not tell her what.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the headquarters, this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the headquarters. A night before Christmas, the Germans came in. Under us were living Christian people, Ukrainians. They were drinking the whole night. And as I was trying to open up the door and go out to look, like, 10 o'clock at night, it was snowing, and I wanted to see if everything is quiet. As I opened the door, I saw two Ukrainian policemen with their guns, already circling around our house. Then I knew something will happen. The whole night, I-- at that time, somehow I always try to sleep at my sister's house. My mother, my other sisters, and my other brothers slept in their house. I did not see them. And I always wanted to be close to my sister, so I stayed there. As we saw already, at night, in the middle of the night was very quiet. And we had a feeling that tomorrow we going to die, because we knew that the police surrounded our house. And we cannot run no more, and this what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Across our door lived a Jewish woman. She was married to a Gentile, a Russian. She had two little daughters. Her husband was in the Red Army. And she came in, and she said to us, what's going to happen to us? But she mentioned even whatever happens, maybe her children will be safe, because they're half-Jewish, because their father is not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: At 5:00 in the morning, it was quiet. That was Christmas. 6 o'clock in the morning was already light a little bit. And the snow was coming down very quiet. And we knew something will happen. After 6:00, like 7 o'clock, we heard already from the center of the town, shooting. Then all of a sudden, I'm looking out from the window, and I see running two Germans and one of the head of the Ukrainian police, Baranchuck, with two guns in his hands. And he's running straight to our house. As they walked up the steps, and they came into the house. And they said-- the German said to my brother-in-law, Zynsztein-- his name was Zynsztein-- you don't worry, and also the [INAUDIBLE] of the police. You your family are going to survive because you are tailors. And we're not going to kill you, but you come down right away with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother-in-law and my sister, they took each other by the hand, and I was walking after them. And as, as we came down from the house, that woman with the two children, they took her out, the Jewish woman. And she started screaming and crying for the Germans, for the Gestapo, that her children are not Jewish. They took her the same way.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we walked down the couple steps from the house, a Ukrainian-- I saw already fire. The Ukrainian policeman-- later on I knew his name was Kobaliuk-- he pull his gun. And a man was running, to run away. He shot him dead right near my legs. I saw the fire of the gun of the bullet, coming through my eyes. As we are going already, I saw this is-- this is the end. And we're walking towards the center of the city of that town, which was only, maybe, about 10 blocks away. The snow was white. And I see bodies laying all over near the houses. The blood on the white snow, all over. And as we come to the center, I see thousands of our people-- Jewish people-- in the center, standing. There's one group on one side, and another group on another side. And as I was running with my sister and my brother-in-law, the head of the Gestapo-- his name was Graf. He was a redhead. He was a cannibal. And he recognized my brother-in-law. And he said, Zynsztein, come here. [GERMAN]. Go on this side. And I saw already, my mother, my sisters, and my brother standing on the other side. They were already on the side, the one which go to die, and the one which go to live. And my mother and my sister were screaming, [INAUDIBLE], come here. She thought that I'm going to go on this side.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the name you were called.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called. And then the Gestapo came over. And he said, you stay this-- on this side, the one who goes to live. Meantime, I see-- I see little wagons with horses, dead bodies already on the horse-- on the wagons, blood all over. And all of a sudden, this same policeman, Ukrainian Kobaliuk runs again near me. I was standing. And I saw a grandmother-- a mother-- no, a mother with her little baby, and her-- and her mother, which was the grandmother. The mother was running with the baby and Kobaliuk, again, puts in the bullets and shots the mother with the baby in the hand. And the mother goes like, oh, oh, and she falls on the snow near me. I see her laying, already dead. The baby falls on the other side, alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the grandmother grab the baby. And then, again, Kobaliuk pulled the trigger, and he shot the grandmother. And the baby, they throw on the wagon, alive. This took up to time-- maybe 12 or 1 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands-- they took us. The ones that are going to survive-- that was tailors, shoemakers, furriers. Altogether, maybe was-- I don't know-- 100 people, 200 people-- exactly amount, I don't know. They told us-- they moved us away from the whole, whole crowd, which must've been, there-- almost 3,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was still in that other--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my sisters and brother also with us-- with the one who goes to survive this time. They were all-- we were all together. And they took us away about five blocks away on another side. We should not see what's going to happen to them. I also saw Dubina, the other policeman. He was big, tall, dark hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a woman-- and I remember her. She was working for the government before. He run over to her. She was running, and he took her-- he was carrying a-- what do you call it? A sword, a long sword. He took out his sword, and he cut off her head. Her head was falling one side, her body on the other side. This, I saw before they took him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were already in the other side. The head of the Gestapo, which they called them the [INAUDIBLE]. Young guy, tall [INAUDIBLE] Nazi, with two others, three others, came over to us. They told us to sit down on the snow and wait. And they gave us a

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: And you will be alive. You will live. And you will work

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We had to sit on that snow until the evening, until 5 or 6 o'clock. At 5, 6 o'clock, we saw already the wagons with the [INAUDIBLE] coming right near us back. All the 3,000 people were taken to a grave out about two miles from the town. That grave was digged months, months before, which we did not know. The grave was big, like a block. They were taken to this grave. Everybody had to get undressed. Ukrainian people were standing and looking from nearby, and grabbing the clothing, whatever they could, from the Jews. Down in the grave was one SS man sitting with a machine gun. They had to undress themselves and walk down steps. When they walked down the steps, he was going through with a machine gun and killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on the grave-- I'll, I'll tell you later in the story when I'm telling how-- we find it out very soon, right after that, because Ukrainian told us, other people. There were still some of them, which was not all murderers. But most of them were collaborating with the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So down in the grave was two men standing there. The Germans giving the orders to put bodies in one side of the grave and in the other side, because they wanted-- had a plan that how much is going to be killed. The grave will be open, and they were always taken-- they knew that the plan more has to go in over there into that grave. So they had to put the bodies-- some of them were still alive, one on top on the other. And they finished the job. They put a white chemical, like to burn against diseases or something. They call it [INAUDIBLE]. Whatever that was, it was a chemical on the bodies. They did not cover the bodies with any ground, so the birds could come and eat their eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time when we saw already [? 5 or ?] 6 o'clock, they came and they told us that we will be taken into two houses and stay there overnight. And then they will give us a couple more, and they will make a little ghetto for us. They pushed us in in two houses with open windows. It must've been 20 below zero-- and packed us in. Ukrainian police, with guns, circled the two houses, and watching us. This was some of the Ukrainian police that I remember them. They-- we went to school together. In the morning, they came in. They told us to get out and they gave us about 10 houses in one of the strips. And they told us, you going to be all right. You're going to live, and everything will be OK. Soon we came there. I don't know how. Yeah. Some of them, some of the Jews were hiding. Some, they knew, so they were running between there were certain places in the building, like a gray build, and they came out. I would say about 500 survived, hiding. And they came into the ghetto. Ukrainian police found out, little by little. But they didn't say anything until they were waiting. We start to work, to do the same thing, to fix the uniforms, to get some food. We were not allowed, only certain times to go to the bazaar out of the ghetto, which was not far away to buy food. And Ukrainians came in, some from the police who had to fix their uniforms and everything. And they were like nothing happened, friendly. And one of the head of the policemen, the one I mentioned before, Baranchuk, took my sister-- the young one, the blonde one. He knew that she knows how to put together a dress. He took her to his house. And she was making dresses for his wife. And also she still worked in the headquarters in the Gestapo. That was going on January, February, 1942. March, one night, my sister comes home again. And she says to my brother-in-law that she heard that they gave a list again-- the Gestapo-- to put who is going to be alive again and who's going to be shot. With the next, they call it [GERMAN]. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Action.

speaker: interviewee
text: Action. In Germany, [GERMAN]. And my sister looked over the list. And she did not see some of my family. She start to scream. She went over right away to the-- back to the Gestapo. And she told them, how come my mother is not there. My brother summoned. And he gave orders right away to put my family back into the list. In the morning after that, again, we were taken out in the ghetto-- all of us, whoever was in the ghetto. And this time came the Gestapo and that Graf, the head of the Gestapo, Ukrainian police, German border police. And they put us in one side, and then they start to read the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us in one side again-- shoemakers, tailors, and the others. And the rest who were not doing this kind of work, they put them in the other side. By accident, I supposed to be with my family. Somehow I got with the one that was supposed to go to the grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we were-- I don't know how many. And I'm already in the line with the one who has to march. So they told us, before they kill you, they're going to cut your head off. So better try to get undressed before. And I already opened up my shirt. All of a sudden, the head of the Gestapo-- that Graf-- saw me, and he put his finger-- he was holding a-- like, you know, they give you a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A machete?

speaker: interviewee
text: A machete, right, And he said, [GERMAN]. On this side. Come here. And as I came close, he gave me a hit. And he told me to go on this side. Otherwise, I would've gone away with the rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1941. I was 11-- between 10 and 11. As I came to the side where my mother was standing. My sisters and I saw a couple-- a husband and wife. This woman was a beautiful woman, and he was a tailor. But somehow the Germans did not like him. And they grabbed him, and he was begging. They took him, but with the rest. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
